Supporters of applied research emphasise accountability. taxpayer money competes with hospitals, education and infrastructure, so governments should seek visible public benefit. mission-driven research can address urgent problems such as climate adaptation, infectious disease or clean energy. What makes these programmes attractive is their ability to connect scientific work with a defined social need. Clear goals also make evaluation easier.

However, practical research depends on earlier discoveries. New treatments and technologies frequently build on basic research whose applications were initially unknown. Public institutions have supported many uncertain projects, yet some have later transformed medicine and industry. Private companies are less likely to fund such work because knowledge spillovers make profits difficult to capture. A narrow policy could also encourage researchers to exaggerate impact in every grant proposal. Only when funders accept uncertainty can genuinely original questions compete with safe, fashionable projects. This does not mean that every field should receive equal money or that researchers should avoid accountability. peer review, ethical standards and regular evaluation remain necessary.

The most effective model is a balanced research portfolio. Governments should set aside resources for urgent missions, stable institutional funding and curiosity-driven research. Had earlier funding focused exclusively on predictable products, many foundational discoveries might never have been made.

In conclusion, practical research should receive strong support because governments must address current needs. Nevertheless, public science should also preserve intellectual diversity and long-term investigation. The value of research lies partly in producing solutions and partly in expanding the knowledge from which future solutions emerge.

Why the exam-length essay is strong

Direct position

The introduction supports urgent practical priorities without excluding exploratory science.

Causal explanation

The essay explains why commercial incentives do not capture every social return.

Developed contrast

Accountability is balanced against uncertainty, discovery and long timescales.

Policy mechanism

A diversified research portfolio connects missions, institutions and intellectual risk.

Recycled language

Earlier collocations return as part of the reasoning rather than as decoration.

Controlled complexity

Advanced grammar remains clear enough for realistic exam conditions.

7. Advanced grammar transformations

1. If governments protected long-term funding, fewer projects would collapse. (Conditional inversion)

Were governments to protect long-term funding, fewer projects would collapse.

2. The agency cancelled grants after researchers had already hired staff. (Past-perfect conditional)

Had the agency reviewed the consequences earlier, it might not have cancelled the grants so abruptly.

3. Stable institutions matter most in scientific funding. (Cleft sentence)

What matters most in scientific funding is institutional stability.

4. Researchers will trust the system only when criteria are transparent. (Negative inversion)

Only when criteria are transparent will researchers trust the system.

5. Public funding supports discovery and trains future scientists. (Not only...but also)

Not only does public funding support discovery, but it also trains future scientists.

6. The programme was designed for innovation, but it became politically controlled. (Participle clause)

Designed for innovation, the programme gradually became politically controlled.

7. Although the project is risky, it may have enormous value. (Fronted concession)

Risky though the project may be, it could have enormous value.

8. The agency should fund research, maintain infrastructure and protect independence. (Controlled parallelism)

The agency should fund research, maintain infrastructure and protect independence.

9. Governments have demanded impact for years, but basic science remains unpredictable. (Present-perfect contrast)

Governments have demanded impact for years, yet basic science remains unpredictable.

10. The university closed the laboratory after funding had ended. (Past perfect)

The university closed the laboratory after its funding had ended.

11. Political appointees lack specialist knowledge, so expert review is necessary. (Nominalisation)

The lack of specialist knowledge among political appointees makes expert review necessary.

12. If funding were more stable, researchers would attempt more ambitious projects. (Conditional inversion)

Researchers would attempt more ambitious projects were funding more stable.

13. Scientists opposed the rule because it threatened academic freedom. (Cleft cause)

It was the threat to academic freedom that caused scientists to oppose the rule.

14. Governments should identify priorities and preserve scientific independence. (Balanced recommendation)

Governments should both identify priorities and preserve scientific independence.

15. The agency introduced the scheme gradually, so institutions had time to adapt. (Participle clause)

Introducing the scheme gradually, the agency gave institutions time to adapt.

16. Researchers changed their methods after replication failed. (Emphatic do)

Researchers did change their methods after replication failed.

17. No resource matters more than a skilled research workforce. (Negative inversion)

Rarely does any resource matter more than a skilled research workforce.

18. The funding system should be competitive, stable and accountable. (Controlled parallelism)

The funding system should encourage competition, preserve stability and ensure accountability.

8. Native Academic Toolbox

1. Upgrade: “The government should spend more on science.” using strategic investment.

Scientific funding should be treated as strategic investment rather than ordinary consumption.

2. Upgrade: “Some projects help other companies too.” using knowledge spillovers.

Knowledge spillovers allow research benefits to extend beyond the original project.

3. Upgrade: “The system funds different kinds of projects.” using portfolio diversity.

Portfolio diversity reduces dependence on one scientific direction.

4. Upgrade: “Young scientists may leave the country.” using brain drain.

Funding instability can accelerate brain drain among early-career researchers.

5. Upgrade: “Politicians should not choose individual grants.” using arms-length funding.

Arms-length funding protects scientific decisions from direct political control.

6. Upgrade: “Universities need stable money.” using institutional funding.

Institutional funding supports permanent staff and shared infrastructure.

7. Upgrade: “The project may help society much later.” using long-term outcomes.

Long-term outcomes may exceed the immediate publications produced by a grant.

8. Upgrade: “Science creates more economic activity.” using economic multiplier.

Research spending can generate an economic multiplier through jobs and innovation.

9. Upgrade: “The agency must explain how it chooses.” using transparent criteria.

Transparent criteria make grant allocation easier to evaluate and contest.

10. Upgrade: “Private firms do not fund every useful idea.” using commercial incentives.

Commercial incentives may neglect socially valuable research without a clear market.

11. Upgrade: “The country is losing research ability.” using scientific capacity.

Repeated cuts can weaken national scientific capacity.

12. Upgrade: “Scientists from several countries work together.” using international collaboration.

International collaboration combines specialised expertise and larger datasets.

13. Upgrade: “The grant system wastes researcher time.” using opportunity cost.

Repeated applications create an opportunity cost by reducing time available for research.

14. Upgrade: “The public should influence broad priorities.” using public consultation.

Public consultation can help define mission-driven research priorities.

15. Upgrade: “The government should not control results.” using scientific independence.

Scientific independence allows evidence to challenge official policy.

9. IELTS Speaking

Part 1 · 15 questions

PART 1 · 1

Were you interested in science at school?

Suggested phrasal verbs
draw onbuild on
Model answer · 89 words

I was interested in science when lessons connected theories with visible experiments. A good teacher could draw on ordinary examples and then build on them gradually. I enjoyed biology and physics more than memorising isolated facts. Those classes also showed me why scientific evidence matters: an explanation should be testable, not merely confident. I probably would have been even more interested if we had discussed how real research project are funded and organised. Practical examples made abstract ideas feel less intimidating and helped me remember the logic behind them.

PART 1 · 2

Do you read science news?

Suggested phrasal verbs
draw onfollow through
Model answer · 85 words

I read major science stories, especially about health, climate and technology. When a headline sounds dramatic, I try to draw on the original study or a reliable explanation. Good reporting should follow through from the result to its limitations instead of presenting one paper as final truth. I also check whether the work involved a small sample, animal research or human trials, because those details change what the finding actually means. I also avoid articles that confuse early findings with advice people should immediately follow.

PART 1 · 3

Have you ever visited a laboratory?

Suggested phrasal verbs
carry outdraw on
Model answer · 85 words

I have visited a teaching laboratory rather than a major research facility. Students were carry out simple experiments and learning to record observations carefully. The instructor explained how professional researchers draw on earlier studies before designing new work. The experience made science feel less like a collection of facts and more like a disciplined process. It also showed how much laboratory equipment and technical support are required even for apparently modest projects. Seeing the equipment also made the financial side of research much more concrete.

PART 1 · 4

Would you work as a scientist?

Suggested phrasal verbs
build onopen up
Model answer · 86 words

I would consider it if the role combined intellectual freedom with reasonable career stability. Scientific work allows people to build on existing knowledge and may open up entirely new questions. However, temporary contracts and funding uncertainty would be difficult for me. I would prefer a research environment where early-career staff had mentoring, predictable income and enough time to investigate important problems rather than constantly preparing the next grant proposal. Career stability would matter because constant funding anxiety can narrow the questions scientists are willing to pursue.

PART 1 · 5

Should ordinary people understand research funding?

Suggested phrasal verbs
draw onset aside
Model answer · 88 words

People do not need to understand every budget line, but they should be able to find out why governments support particular fields. Governments should set aside time to explain decisions that draw on scientific evidence, and citizens deserve clear information about priorities, costs and expected public benefit. This matters because research competes with other forms of public expenditure. Better explanation can strengthen public confidence without pretending that every project will produce an immediate product. Clear public explanations also make political debates about priorities more informed and less symbolic.

PART 1 · 6

Do you trust scientific studies?

Suggested phrasal verbs
draw onphase out
Model answer · 89 words

I generally trust well-conducted research, but I do not treat a single paper as unquestionable. I draw on the methods, sample and whether other studies support the result. Strong conclusions usually draw on several pieces of evidence rather than one striking experiment. peer review and replication reduce error, although neither system is perfect. Trust should come from transparent methods and correction, not from the idea that scientists never make mistakes. I am more cautious when the study is funded by an interested company and the conflict is poorly disclosed.

PART 1 · 7

Would you join a citizen-science project?

Suggested phrasal verbs
step incarry out
Model answer · 83 words

I would step in for a well-designed citizen science project, especially one involving biodiversity or local air quality. Volunteers can carry out observations across places that professional teams could not cover alone. The project should provide clear instructions and explain how the data will be checked. Public participation is most meaningful when contributors can see the final research outcomes rather than simply supplying free labour without feedback. I would especially value a project that taught volunteers how uncertainty and data quality are handled.

PART 1 · 8

Is science well funded where you live?

Suggested phrasal verbs
cut backhold back
Model answer · 78 words

Funding seems uneven. Some high-profile fields receive support, while smaller laboratories face uncertainty and outdated equipment. When agencies cut back abruptly, they can hold back projects that already required years of preparation. I understand that governments have limited budgets, but unstable decisions waste earlier investment. funding continuity is particularly important for long observations, doctoral students and technical staff whose expertise cannot be replaced quickly. Sudden cuts can therefore destroy capacity even when the headline saving appears relatively small.

PART 1 · 9

Should companies fund university research?

Suggested phrasal verbs
scale upspin off
Model answer · 84 words

Companies should help fund university research, especially when discoveries are close to practical use. Industry can scale up promising technologies and support firms that spin off from universities. The risk is that commercial incentives may narrow the questions researchers pursue or delay publication. Strong agreements should protect academic freedom, disclosure and access to results. Public money is still necessary for work with uncertain or distant commercial returns. Universities should also publish contracts so the public can see whether commercial partners influence methods or publication.

PART 1 · 10

Would you donate to medical research?

Suggested phrasal verbs
pay offbuild on
Model answer · 77 words

I would donate to a trustworthy organisation with transparent accounts and clear scientific priorities. medical research can build on earlier discoveries and eventually pay off through treatments, diagnostics or prevention. However, charities should explain whether donations support basic science, patient services or one specific disease. Emotional campaigns are understandable, but good funding should also consider neglected conditions and the wider research ecosystem. Long-term benefits matter because many discoveries support several treatments rather than one easily advertised cure.

PART 1 · 11

Do scientists explain their work clearly?

Suggested phrasal verbs
draw onpour into
Model answer · 77 words

Some scientists explain research extremely well, while others use technical language that excludes the public. A good communicator can draw on a method without pretending it is simpler than it really is. They should also draw on concrete examples and state uncertainty honestly. transparent communication matters because misunderstanding can weaken institutional trust, especially when scientific advice changes as new evidence appears. Scientists also need time and incentives for communication instead of being judged only by specialist publications.

PART 1 · 12

Should failed experiments receive funding?

Suggested phrasal verbs
follow throughbuild on
Model answer · 88 words

Failure is part of research, so funding should not depend only on positive results. Researchers must still follow through on careful methods and report what happened. A failed experiment can build on knowledge by showing which approach does not work. The problem arises when projects repeatedly fail because of weak design or poor management. Funders should evaluate learning, openness and research integrity, not demand success that no honest scientist can guarantee. Negative results should be accessible so other teams do not waste money repeating the same hidden failure.

PART 1 · 13

Do you prefer practical or theoretical science?

Suggested phrasal verbs
build onpay off
Model answer · 80 words

I find practical applications easier to appreciate, but theoretical science is essential because applications often build on ideas developed much earlier. basic research may not pay off immediately, yet it can transform technology decades later. The best system supports both curiosity-driven research and work aimed at current social problems. Requiring every scientist to predict a product would discourage the unusual questions from which major discoveries sometimes emerge. This balance protects the innovation pipeline from becoming too narrow or politically fashionable.

PART 1 · 14

Would you study abroad for research?

Suggested phrasal verbs
open updraw on
Model answer · 77 words

I would consider studying abroad if a programme offered specialised supervision and a strong international network. Researchers often draw on equipment, datasets and expertise that no single institution possesses. Moving abroad could help me open up a new field, although funding and visas would matter. international collaboration is valuable because scientific problems such as climate change and infectious disease do not respect national borders. International experience can also improve methods by exposing researchers to different scientific cultures.

PART 1 · 15

Should scientists share their data?

Suggested phrasal verbs
open uphold back
Model answer · 84 words

Scientists should share research data when privacy, security and consent allow it. Open datasets can open up new questions and make replication easier. However, immediate release may hold back researchers who spent years collecting unique information if others can publish first. A fair system should recognise data creation, provide clear citation rules and use careful data governance. Sharing should expand knowledge without ignoring ethical limits or legitimate researcher effort. Shared data should include documentation, because a large dataset without context can easily be misunderstood.

Part 3 · 15 questions

PART 3 · 1

Why should governments fund basic research?

Suggested phrasal verbs
pour intopay off
Model answer · 131 words

Governments should pour into basic research because markets often underinvest in work whose benefits are distant, uncertain or widely shared. A company may not finance a discovery that competitors can also use, even when the eventual social return is enormous. Public support creates a diverse research portfolio and allows scientists to investigate questions before a commercial application exists. Some projects will fail, but others may pay off through medicines, materials or technologies that no committee could have predicted. The key is not to promise immediate profit from every grant, but to maintain portfolio diversity, strong peer review and enough funding continuity for difficult ideas to develop. This funding also supports methods, datasets and trained researchers that later projects can reuse, even when the original question does not produce a headline discovery.

PART 3 · 2

Should research funding focus on national priorities?

Suggested phrasal verbs
set asidebuild on
Model answer · 127 words

Governments should set aside significant resources for urgent priorities such as climate, health and energy security. mission-driven research can coordinate institutions and accelerate practical progress. However, a system focused only on current political goals may neglect the basic research on which future solutions will build on. Priorities also change, whereas scientific capacity takes years to develop. A balanced model would support strategic missions while preserving independent, curiosity-driven research. Funding agencies should publish transparent criteria and allow researchers to challenge narrow definitions of relevance. National priorities matter, but they should guide part of the portfolio rather than convert the entire scientific system into a short-term government consultancy. Mission programmes should be reviewed periodically, because political enthusiasm can persist after the original objective has become unrealistic or scientifically outdated.

PART 3 · 3

Is peer review the best way to allocate grants?

Suggested phrasal verbs
draw onstep in
Model answer · 128 words

peer review remains valuable because specialists can draw on detailed knowledge that politicians or general administrators do not possess. Reviewers can assess methods, feasibility and how a proposal relates to existing work. Nevertheless, the system may favour established institutions, fashionable topics and applicants skilled at grant writing. Panels can also reproduce disciplinary assumptions. Funding agencies should therefore step in with safeguards such as diverse reviewers, conflict declarations and partial lotteries among equally strong proposals. human judgement should remain central, but decisions need data on success rates, regional concentration and reviewer consistency. Peer review is not perfect; it is a tool that requires transparency, experimentation and regular evaluation. Some agencies are also testing anonymised review and partial lotteries, which may reduce prestige effects when several proposals are similarly strong.

PART 3 · 4

What are the risks of political interference in science?

Suggested phrasal verbs
phase outshut down
Model answer · 134 words

political interference can redirect money from scientific merit towards ideological loyalty or immediate publicity. A government may phase out work that produces inconvenient findings or shut down projects after years of investment. This weakens academic freedom, deters international collaboration and encourages researchers to avoid controversial questions. The harm extends beyond one grant cycle because teams disperse, equipment becomes idle and young scientists leave. Elected governments have a legitimate role in setting broad budgets and priorities, but individual awards should normally be protected through arms-length funding. democratic accountability requires oversight of agencies, not political selection of results. Science cannot serve the public if evidence must first satisfy the preferences of those in power. Researchers must remain able to publish inconvenient results, otherwise public funding becomes a mechanism for producing official confirmation rather than independent knowledge.

PART 3 · 5

Can private investment replace public science funding?

Suggested phrasal verbs
scale upstep in
Model answer · 126 words

private investment is excellent at helping promising discoveries scale up, especially when a clear product and market exist. Companies can move quickly, combine engineering with marketing and accept risks in return for profit. However, they rarely replace the need for governments to step in at the earliest stages. basic research, rare diseases and public datasets may generate large knowledge spillovers without a reliable revenue stream. Commercial priorities can also narrow access or keep results proprietary. The strongest model combines public discovery with responsible public-private partnerships. Private finance should complement, not substitute for, the institutional funding, training and open knowledge on which later commercial innovation depends. Public funders also support standards, shared facilities and training that private firms benefit from but have little incentive to finance individually.

PART 3 · 6

How can research funding become more equitable?

Suggested phrasal verbs
open upset aside
Model answer · 122 words

Funding systems can open up opportunity by reducing unnecessary administrative barriers and publishing clear feedback. Agencies may set aside resources for early-career researchers, underfunded regions or institutions with weaker infrastructure. This is not the same as abandoning excellence; talent cannot demonstrate excellence without equipment, mentoring and time. equitable access also requires support for applicants who lack large grant offices. However, targeted programmes should have measurable outcomes and should not isolate recipients from mainstream competitions. A fair system combines scientific quality with capacity building, transparent data and recognition that previous funding success often creates the conditions for future success. Agencies should publish application and award data by career stage, institution and region so that hidden concentration can be identified rather than merely suspected.

PART 3 · 7

Should governments fund research with no immediate use?

Suggested phrasal verbs
pay offfollow through
Model answer · 127 words

Yes, because the absence of an immediate use does not mean the absence of value. Scientific breakthroughs often emerge from questions that initially appeared remote from practical needs. Governments must follow through on long-term commitments because curiosity-driven research may require years before patterns become visible. Some work will never pay off commercially, but it can improve methods, train scientists or prevent society from relying on a narrow range of ideas. The difficulty is accountability. Researchers should report progress, share data where appropriate and explain why the question remains important. Public funding should tolerate uncertainty, but not secrecy, careless methods or endless continuation without review. Funding portfolios also need room for replication and theory, because an obsession with visible novelty can produce fragile results and repeated scientific fashions.

PART 3 · 8

How do funding cuts affect young scientists?

Suggested phrasal verbs
cut backhold back
Model answer · 119 words

When agencies cut back grants, early-career researchers often lose contracts, supervision and access to equipment first. Senior scientists may have permanent positions or established networks, whereas doctoral students and postdoctoral researchers depend on one project. This can hold back scientific careers at the moment when people are deciding whether to remain in research. A prolonged shock may create brain drain and a missing generation of mentors. The consequences continue after budgets recover because laboratories cannot instantly rebuild teams. Targeted bridge funding, longer contracts and protection for doctoral training can preserve the research workforce during temporary disruption. Universities should avoid shifting every funding risk onto junior staff through extremely short contracts that make long-term research and personal planning almost impossible.

PART 3 · 9

Does competition improve scientific quality?

Suggested phrasal verbs
draw onopen up
Model answer · 127 words

Competition can improve proposals because researchers must clarify methods, significance and alternatives. Panels draw on expert knowledge to compare limited options, and competitive calls may open up fields to new ideas. However, very low success rates waste enormous time and encourage conservative projects that appear safe to reviewers. Competition also rewards institutions with professional grant support. A healthy system needs both competitive grants and stable institutional funding. Core money maintains staff, facilities and risky early work, while competition supports selected projects. The aim should be productive comparison, not a lottery in which most researchers repeatedly rewrite similar applications instead of conducting science. Reviewers should also be compensated or given workload credit, since the system depends on expert labour that is often treated as an unlimited free resource.

PART 3 · 10

Should scientific data be freely available?

Suggested phrasal verbs
open uphold back
Model answer · 121 words

open science and accessible research data can open up replication, education and new analysis. Publicly funded results should normally be available to the public that financed them. Nevertheless, immediate openness can conflict with patient privacy, national security or the legitimate effort of teams that collected unique data. Poorly designed mandates may hold back participation if communities fear misuse. Strong data governance, consent and controlled-access systems can balance these concerns. Openness should include methods, code and negative results where possible, but it must be organised rather than treated as uploading everything without context or responsibility. Public access should include understandable summaries, because technically open material is not genuinely accessible when only specialists can interpret it. Careful licensing can also protect vulnerable participants.

PART 3 · 11

How can scientists rebuild public trust?

Suggested phrasal verbs
draw onfollow through
Model answer · 126 words

Scientists need to draw on clear examples, disclose uncertainty and explain how conclusions change when evidence improves. Public trust is weakened when experts appear dismissive or communicate only after a crisis. Institutions must also follow through on corrections and acknowledge conflicts of interest. transparent communication is necessary, but trust depends on behaviour as well as language: fair access, honest methods and visible research integrity. Citizen participation can help, especially when communities contribute to questions and data collection. Scientists should not promise certainty they do not possess. A trustworthy institution demonstrates how disagreement is resolved and why revision is a strength rather than proof that research is useless. Scientists can also involve communities earlier when defining questions, particularly where research affects local environments, health or historical experience.

PART 3 · 12

Are large research facilities worth the cost?

Suggested phrasal verbs
pour intoscale up
Model answer · 120 words

Major instruments and laboratories can be extremely expensive, but they support work that individual universities cannot perform alone. Governments may pour into research infrastructure because advanced imaging, particle facilities or long-term observatories create shared national capacity. These facilities also train specialists and help firms scale up materials or medical discoveries. The risk is concentration: a few prestigious centres may absorb funds while smaller institutions decline. Decisions should examine usage, industry access, scientific output and opportunity cost. Large facilities are justified when they serve broad communities, maintain unique capability and fit a coherent long-term strategy rather than functioning mainly as political monuments. Shared facilities require predictable operating budgets; expensive construction has little value if later cuts leave instruments unavailable or understaffed.

PART 3 · 13

Should unsuccessful research projects be considered wasteful?

Suggested phrasal verbs
carry outbuild on
Model answer · 119 words

An unsuccessful result is not automatically waste. Scientists carry out experiments precisely because the answer is unknown. A negative finding can prevent others repeating the same path and allow later work to build on more accurate assumptions. Waste occurs when methods are careless, results remain hidden or projects continue despite clear failure. Funding agencies should reward honest reporting and replication studies, not only positive headlines. research outcomes include skills, datasets and improved techniques as well as discoveries. If every project had to guarantee success, public funding would select only predictable work and would abandon the uncertainty that makes genuine research necessary. Publishing negative findings and methods can turn apparent failure into a useful contribution to the wider research ecosystem.

PART 3 · 14

How should international research be funded?

Suggested phrasal verbs
set asidefollow through
Model answer · 118 words

Global problems require international collaboration, shared datasets and teams with complementary expertise. Governments should set aside funds for cross-border research and follow through on multi-year commitments, since abrupt national changes can damage partners elsewhere. Agreements need clear rules on data, intellectual property and ethical review. Wealthier countries should avoid designing collaborations that use lower-income partners merely as data suppliers. Strong partnerships build local scientific capacity and share authorship, training and infrastructure. International funding can appear politically vulnerable, but disease, climate and biodiversity do not stop at national borders. Cooperation is therefore a practical necessity, not a charitable extra. Funding agreements should also protect researchers from sudden political restrictions that make lawful cross-border collaboration impossible halfway through a project.

PART 3 · 15

What should count as a successful research policy?

Suggested phrasal verbs
pay offspin off
Model answer · 122 words

A successful policy should create reliable knowledge, skilled people and the capacity to respond to future problems. Some projects may spin off companies or pay off through measurable products, but commercial output is only one indicator. Governments should track research integrity, international influence, training, data resources and long-term outcomes. They should also examine regional and distributional effects. A system that produces a few spectacular patents while weakening universities and early-career pathways may not be sustainable. Research policy succeeds when it supports a diverse innovation pipeline, informs public decisions and preserves enough independence for scientists to discover results that policymakers did not expect. Success therefore requires balance between discovery, application and resilience rather than one narrow ranking based on patents or publication counts.

10. Five IELTS Writing Task 2 topics

Before writing: check that each body paragraph has a clear topic sentence, explanation, development and a relevant consequence or example. Your position must remain consistent from the introduction to the conclusion.
TASK 2 · 1

Universities should receive stable government funding rather than compete for most research money through grants. To what extent do you agree or disagree?

Optional collocation bank
research infrastructureearly-career researcherscompetitive grantsinstitutional fundingtransparent criteriameasurable outcomesresearch integritycarry outopen up
Model essay · 349 words

Universities need both stable institutional support and competitive grants. I largely agree that a greater share should come through predictable government funding, although competition remains necessary for selected projects.

Stable funding maintains research infrastructure, technical staff and long-term datasets. These resources serve many teams and cannot be rebuilt each time a grant ends. What competitive systems often overlook is the permanent capacity required before a successful proposal can be written. Core support also protects early-career researchers from extreme dependence on one short project. However, automatic funding can reduce pressure to improve and may preserve weak programmes indefinitely. competitive grants allow new ideas and researchers to challenge established institutions. Panels can compare methods and direct money towards promising work.

The correct balance is therefore mixed. Governments should finance laboratories, doctoral training and essential staff through institutional funding, while using grants for specific questions and expansion. Only when core capacity is secure can competition reward scientific quality rather than existing institutional wealth. Agencies should publish transparent criteria and track measurable outcomes. Stable support should not be unconditional. Universities must demonstrate research integrity, responsible spending and access to shared facilities. Periodic external review can identify departments that need reform.

A further reason for stable support is efficiency. Researchers currently spend substantial time rewriting similar applications and waiting for decisions. Extremely competitive systems can therefore reduce the amount of science produced even when they appear selective. Core funding would allow teams to carry out pilot work, maintain datasets and prepare stronger applications. Regional diversity should also be considered. If grants repeatedly flow to universities that already possess large laboratories and professional proposal offices, other institutions struggle to develop. A modest capacity-building element can open up opportunity without guaranteeing permanent support. Funding agencies should publish concentration data and examine whether excellence is being measured independently of inherited resources. Stable and competitive funding should therefore be viewed as complementary layers rather than rival systems.

In conclusion: universities should not compete for nearly every research cost. Predictable funding creates resilience and intellectual freedom, while competitive grants remain valuable for selecting additional projects and encouraging new ideas.

TASK 2 · 2

Some people believe private companies are better than governments at funding useful research. Others think public funding is essential. Discuss both views and give your opinion.

Optional collocation bank
commercial incentivesscale upspin offbasic researchknowledge spilloversstep inresearch infrastructuredoctoral trainingdemocratic accountability
Model essay · 346 words

Private companies and governments fund research for different reasons. Businesses are often efficient at developing useful products, while public agencies support work whose benefits are uncertain or widely shared. In my view, both are necessary, but public funding provides the foundation.

Companies have strong commercial incentives to identify customer needs, control costs and scale up successful discoveries. They can move rapidly from a prototype to manufacturing and may spin off specialised firms. This makes private investment particularly effective in applied research. However, companies may ignore basic research, rare diseases or environmental problems without a profitable market. The benefits of knowledge frequently spread through knowledge spillovers, so one company cannot capture the full return. Were science left entirely to private investors, socially valuable questions with weak revenue would receive too little support.

Public agencies can step in by funding early discovery, shared data and research infrastructure. They also support doctoral training and independent evidence that may challenge industry. Nevertheless, government programmes require democratic accountability and protection from political interference. The strongest arrangement is a transparent partnership. Public money should support foundational work, while companies help with engineering, trials and distribution. Agreements must protect publication and fair access.

Public funding also protects research that produces evidence inconvenient to commercial sponsors. A company developing a product may have little incentive to finance independent studies of environmental damage or weak effectiveness. Universities supported through public money can carry out this work and provide scientific advice to regulators. At the same time, public agencies can learn from industry. Long approval processes and fragmented responsibility may delay research translation. Partnerships should define milestones, publication rights and public access from the beginning. If taxpayers supported the underlying discovery, pricing and licensing arrangements should recognise that contribution rather than treating the final innovation as entirely private. Public oversight should focus on access, integrity and value while leaving technical judgments to independent expertise.

In conclusion: businesses may fund some practical research more efficiently, but they cannot replace the broad scientific ecosystem supported by government. Public and private investment should complement rather than imitate each other.

TASK 2 · 3

Governments increasingly direct research funding towards a small number of national missions. Do the advantages outweigh the disadvantages?

Optional collocation bank
pour intoresearch translationmeasurable outcomesbasic researchset asidecuriosity-driven researchinternational collaborationapplied researchresearch ecosystem
Model essay · 322 words

National research missions concentrate money and institutions on defined goals such as clean energy, cancer or pandemic preparedness. This approach offers important advantages, but I believe it is beneficial only when it does not crowd out broader science.

The main advantage is coordination. A mission can pour into shared facilities, data and multidisciplinary teams. Clear objectives attract political attention and make research translation easier. What missions provide is a common direction that fragmented grants may lack. They can also create public legitimacy. Citizens understand why governments support research connected to an urgent problem. Defined targets make it easier to assess measurable outcomes and identify delays.

The disadvantage is concentration. Political leaders may select fashionable goals while neglecting the basic research needed for future solutions. Large programmes can also become bureaucratic, protecting their budgets after evidence changes. Governments have frequently announced ambitious missions, yet not all have developed the institutional capacity required to deliver them. A balanced portfolio should set aside mission funding while preserving curiosity-driven research, replication and stable university support. Independent review should assess whether a mission remains scientifically plausible.

Missions can also strengthen international collaboration when countries agree on shared standards and contribute complementary facilities. This is useful for problems whose scale exceeds one university or nation. However, governments may use mission language to centralise control and reward politically favoured institutions. The solution is staged evaluation. Independent panels should review scientific progress, management and alternative approaches. If a programme no longer justifies its cost, money should be redirected rather than protected because leaders have attached prestige to the original announcement. A mission should organise risk, not eliminate intellectual diversity. This would preserve urgency without allowing one national slogan to dominate the entire research ecosystem.

In conclusion: national missions can accelerate progress and communicate purpose. Their advantages outweigh the disadvantages only when funding remains diverse and agencies are willing to revise priorities rather than treating political slogans as permanent scientific strategy.

TASK 2 · 4

Young researchers often leave science because of short contracts and uncertain funding. What problems does this cause, and what solutions should governments and universities introduce?

Optional collocation bank
funding uncertaintyearly-career researchersbrain drainresearch workforcepublication pressuretargeted supportfunding cutsequitable accessbasic research
Model essay · 320 words

Short contracts and funding uncertainty push many talented researchers out of science. This creates problems for individuals, universities and the wider economy.

The first problem is lost expertise. early-career researchers develop specialised methods and international networks, but they may leave before becoming independent scientists. Laboratories then lose continuity, while students lose mentors. A widespread departure can create brain drain and weaken the future research workforce. The second problem is intellectual conservatism. Researchers who must secure the next contract may avoid risky projects and controversial questions. What short-term funding rewards is constant application activity rather than patient investigation. This can reduce originality and increase publication pressure.

Governments should provide bridge grants, longer fellowships and stable support for technical staff. Universities should limit the use of repeated short contracts and offer transparent career criteria. targeted support is particularly important during sudden funding cuts. Agencies can also reduce administrative waste by coordinating application formats and reporting requirements. Not only would this save researcher time, but it would also make scientific careers more accessible to people without financial support from their families.

The personal effects are also substantial. Researchers may postpone housing, family decisions and medical care because the next contract is uncertain. This narrows the profession towards people who can tolerate instability or rely on family wealth, weakening equitable access and intellectual diversity. Universities should treat postdoctoral work as employment, not indefinite apprenticeship. Salaries, notice periods and professional development should reflect that reality. Funding agencies can require career plans and limit the proportion of a laboratory supported through extremely short appointments. These changes would not guarantee permanent academic jobs, but they would make transitions more honest and manageable. Clear pathways into industry, government and teaching can also show that scientific training remains valuable outside a permanent university post.

In conclusion: insecure careers disperse knowledge and discourage ambitious science. Longer funding periods, bridge support and clearer employment pathways are necessary to preserve scientific capacity.

TASK 2 · 5

Why has public trust in science weakened in some countries? How can research institutions rebuild it?

Optional collocation bank
open sciencecitizen sciencefollow throughresearch integritybasic researchapplied researchresearch ecosystemgrant fundingpeer review
Model essay · 328 words

Public trust in science has weakened partly because research is communicated through dramatic headlines, political conflict and changing advice. The problem is not simply ignorance; institutions have sometimes failed to explain uncertainty and conflicts of interest.

A single study may be presented as a final answer, while later correction appears to be contradiction. During crises, experts may communicate with excessive confidence because they fear confusion. Had institutions explained uncertainty earlier, later changes might have appeared as learning rather than failure. Trust also declines when funding relationships are unclear. Commercial sponsorship, political pressure or inaccessible data can make research appear aligned with powerful interests. Social groups that have experienced discrimination may reasonably question whether institutions serve them.

Research organisations should use transparent communication, publish methods and support open science. Scientists need training and time to explain evidence in ordinary language. citizen science and community partnerships can involve people before results are announced. However, communication alone is insufficient. Institutions must follow through on corrections, enforce research integrity and disclose conflicts. Only when behaviour matches public claims can institutional trust be rebuilt.

Media incentives also contribute. Preliminary findings receive attention because novelty is newsworthy, whereas replication and correction attract less coverage. When claims later weaken, the public may conclude that scientists are unreliable rather than recognising that revision is part of the process. Institutions should publish plain-language summaries, funding sources and confidence levels. They should also make experts available outside emergencies, when relationships can develop without immediate political pressure. Community advisory groups can help identify concerns that researchers might otherwise dismiss. Trust is not created by repeating the word “evidence”; it grows through repeated fair and competent interaction. Research organisations must also respond visibly when misconduct occurs, because silence protects reputation only briefly and damages public confidence later.

In conclusion: trust has weakened because scientific uncertainty is poorly communicated and institutions sometimes appear remote or interested. Openness, participation and visible accountability can rebuild confidence without pretending that science offers permanent certainty.

Course index · Module 1 · Chapter 08

Academic English Studio · Topic 08 · Scientific Research and Public Funding


Saved automatically on this device.
